attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-year history of persistent itching and darkened patches on her upper back. The itch began before the skin changes appeared. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She initially tried using over the counter 1% hydrocortisone cream and fungal cream with no change to the patches. She works as an office administrator and denies any new exposures or changes to her routine. She was born in Australia and her parents migrated from the Philippines fifty years ago,

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are symmetrical, rippled, red- brownish patches on her upper back between her shoulder blades. The affected areas are mildly hyperpigmented with a reticulated pattern. There is no scaling, excoriation, or other skin changes elsewhere on examination.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of cutaneous amyloidosis, specifically the macular variant. The amyloid material is deposited in the papillary dermis. Cutaneous amyloidosis typically presents with rippled or reticulated hyperpigmented patches on the upper back, often between the shoulder blades and often presents when patients are in their 40s and with skin phototype III-IV (people from Asia, Middle East and South America). A characteristic feature is pruritus that precedes the visible skin changes, as seen in this case. Other types of cutaneous amyloidosis include nodular amyloidosis (multiple amber or brown nodules over limbs, head and neck) and lichen amyloidosis (red-brown papules on the anterior legs and less on the upper limbs, back and head).

The diagnosis of macular amyloidosis can be confirmed by skin biopsy with Congo red staining, which demonstrates deposits of amyloid in the papillary dermis. The cause is unknown but rarely can be familial. Treatment is challenging and focuses on symptomatic relief of pruritus with topical steroids, antihistamines, and phototherapy.

Tinea versicolor is a common fungal infection caused by Malassezia species that presents with hypo- or hyperpigmented patches with fine scaling. Unlike this case, it typically responds to antifungal treatment and does not have the characteristic rippled appearance.

Confluent and reticulated papillomatosis (Gougerot-Carteaud syndrome) presents with brown, velvety patches and plaques typically in the intermammary and interscapular regions. However, it has a more velvety texture and lacks the intense pruritus seen in this case.

Acanthosis nigricans presents as dark, velvety plaques typically in the flexural areas such as the neck, axillae, and groin. It is often associated with insulin resistance and obesity, which are not features of this case.

Macular lichen planus typically presents with polygonal, flat-topped, violaceous papules and plaques, often with white streaks (Wickham's striae). It can be intensely pruritic but does not typically present with the rippled pattern seen in this case.
learningPoints: Macular amyloidosis typically presents with pruritic rippled hyperpigmented patches in the upper back.
Skin biopsy with Congo red staining is diagnostic for cutaneous amyloidosis.
references: Ngan V, Aung T, Coulson I.  Cutaneous amyloidosis. Dermnet NZ.  2022.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/cutaneous-amyloidosis

Bandhlish A, Aggarwal A, Koranne RV. A clinico-epidemiological study of macular amyloidosis from north India. Indian J Dermatol. 2012 Jul;57(4):269-74.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3401840/
